key_points: Drainage refers to the river system of an area including the main river and its tributaries.
A drainage basin is the area drained by a river and its tributaries while a water divide separates two drainage basins.
India’s drainage system is classified into Himalayan rivers and Peninsular rivers.
Himalayan rivers are perennial as they receive water from both rainfall and melting glaciers.
Peninsular rivers are mostly seasonal and depend mainly on monsoon rainfall.
The Indus, Ganga and Brahmaputra form the three major Himalayan river systems.
The Brahmaputra forms braided channels and river islands due to heavy sediment deposition.
Peninsular rivers such as Godavari, Krishna, Mahanadi and Kaveri generally flow eastwards into the Bay of Bengal.
Rivers like Narmada and Tapi flow westwards through rift valleys into the Arabian Sea.
Rivers play an important role in agriculture, transportation, hydroelectric power generation and human settlements.
examples: Example of a rift valley river: Narmada
Example of east flowing river: Krishna
Example of lagoon in India: Chilika Lake
Example of glacial lake: Dal Lake
Example of saltwater lake: Sambhar Lake
formulas: Drainage system = Network of rivers and tributaries in a region
Perennial river = River fed by glaciers and rainfall throughout the year
Seasonal river = River mainly dependent on monsoon rainfall
Delta = Landform formed by sediment deposition at river mouth
exam_tips: Learn the sources and courses of major rivers like Indus, Ganga and Brahmaputra.
Understand the differences between Himalayan and Peninsular rivers.
Remember examples of east-flowing and west-flowing rivers.
Study the formation of landforms such as meanders, oxbow lakes and deltas.
Focus on economic importance of rivers and the causes of river pollution.
